title: What's the Right Way to Email a College Professor?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You need to ask your professor a question. You open a new email. Your hands hover over the keyboard. What do you say? 'Hey' feels too casual. 'Dear Professor' feels too formal. You don't want to sound rude. You don't want to sound needy. Here is the simple etiquette that professors actually want you to follow.

The Anatomy of a Good Email

Every professional email has five parts. Include all of them.

Subject line: Be specific. 'Question about Friday's lecture' is good. 'Help' is bad. 'Chem 101 — office hours request' is better. Include the course name and number.

Greeting: 'Dear Professor [Last Name]' is always safe. If they've told you to call them by their first name, use that. Otherwise, stick with 'Professor.' Never start with 'Hey' or 'Hi [First Name]' unless you know them well.

Introduction: Remind them who you are. 'I'm in your Tuesday/Thursday CHEM 101 class.' Professors have hundreds of students. They don't memorize names. Help them.

Body: State your question or request clearly and politely. 'I have a question about the problem set due Friday. On question 3, I'm not sure how to apply the formula for molarity. Could you clarify?' Be specific. Vague questions get vague answers.

Closing: 'Thank you for your time,' 'Best regards,' or 'Sincerely.' Then your full name.

The Wrong Way

Don't send: 'Hey, I missed class. What did I miss?' This is rude. It asks the professor to summarize hours of material for you. It shows no effort on your part.

Don't send: 'I need an extension.' Without context, this is demanding. Professors are more likely to grant extensions when you explain why and ask politely.

Don't send: 'When are your office hours?' This is on the syllabus. Look there first. If you can't find it, say 'I checked the syllabus but couldn't find your office hours. Could you let me know?' This shows effort.

Don't send a text message. 'R u in ur office 2day?' This is not email. This is a text. Professors are not your friends. Email professionally.

The Golden Rule: Be Specific

Bad: 'I'm confused about the assignment.'
Good: 'I'm confused about question 3 on the problem set. I understand how to find the derivative, but I'm not sure how to apply the chain rule to the second term. Could you explain that step?'

The good email shows you tried. It shows exactly where you're stuck. The professor can answer quickly. They appreciate this.

When to Email vs. When to Go to Office Hours

Email is for: quick questions, clarifications, scheduling appointments, letting the professor know you'll be absent.

Office hours are for: complex questions, discussing essay ideas, going over a test you failed, building a relationship for recommendation letters.

If your question will take more than two sentences to answer, go to office hours. Don't expect professors to teach you over email.

The 24-Hour Rule

Professors are busy. They may not reply immediately. Wait 24 hours before following up. If you haven't heard back, send a brief follow-up. 'I wanted to follow up on my email from yesterday about question 3. Thank you for your time.'

Don't email again after two hours. Don't send multiple emails in a row. Don't show up at their office demanding a reply. Be patient.

Proofread Before Sending

Typos happen. But an email full of errors looks careless. Read your email out loud before sending. Check that you spelled the professor's name correctly. Check that you included the course number. Check that you said 'please' and 'thank you.'

A professional email takes two minutes to write. It can make the difference between a helpful reply and an annoyed one. Take the time.
